danger_signals.meaning: Direct threat to player; collision causes damage/death

danger_signals.visual_form: Underwater setting with submerged continents and ocean landscape
danger_signals.meaning: World state catastrophe—polar ice caps melted; indicates invasion success and environmental collapse
Mechanical danger—running out of fuel results in loss of life

danger_signals.visual_form: Human prisoners held in alien facilities requiring rescue
danger_signals.meaning: Stakes indicator; humans are under threat from alien 'man-ham livestock' farming

safety_signals.visual_form: Land masses where Sqoon submarine docks
safety_signals.meaning: Safe zones for discharging rescued humans; triggers power-up and fuel replenishment

safety_signals.visual_form: Aquatic female entities encountered during levels
safety_signals.meaning: Non-hostile NPCs; collision triggers hidden bonus messages indicating positive world-state outcome

safety_signals.visual_form: End-of-level facilities destroyed by ice ball bombs
safety_signals.meaning: Mission progress; destroying all bases leads to alien surrender (victory state)

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Military authority requesting aid via distress signal

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Blue-screen message appearance in Japanese version vs. threat message in US version
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Underwater alien bases; facilities housing captured humans; banners carried by aliens during level transitions
Player-controlled submarine as faction representative; reluctant hero character

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Not directly shown; implied through distress signal context
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: General of Earth Defense Army is desperate enough to beg a pirate for aid

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Entire world submerged underwater; polar ice caps melted; continents sunk
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Humans captured in alien facilities; submarine warfare becomes necessary survival mechanism

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Alien facilities intact; banners carried by aliens indicating control
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Aliens continue offensive operations; humans remain imprisoned

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Player victory (bases destroyed, all loops completed)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Banner message showing alien surrender; blue-screen message (Japanese: 'We give up... We surrender. You're GREAT. Ottoto')
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Aliens cease hostile operations; prisoners freed; faction surrender

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Blue-screen threat message appears regardless in US version despite alien concession
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Ambiguous ending; aliens surrender but threat persists (regional difference design)

environmental_storytelling.example: Entire visible game world is underwater—visually communicates that Earth has been catastrophically altered by alien attack (melted polar ice caps, sunken continents)
Alien bases contain captured humans; destroying these bases through gameplay reveals both enemy infrastructure and stakes (human liberation)
Non-hostile mermaids and hidden bonus messages suggest mythological/fantastical elements coexisting with sci-fi invasion narrative; indicate hope and ally presence
Japanese version specifies Pentagon as distress origin, establishing geopolitical context; US version removes this specificity (regional localization choice)
Game contains three distinct loops; completing all loops with all bases destroyed triggers ultimate victory state, suggesting ongoing alien threat that requires sustained player effort

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Navigates underwater environment, fires missiles at enemies, deploys ice ball bombs against facilities
Must reach island within 60 seconds or player loses life; creates tension-driven navigation
Must discharge rescued humans at island to continue rescue operations; enforces strategic gameplay loop
Continue offensive operations; carry banners signaling dominance; maintain captured human facilities
Japanese version: surrender explicitly via blue-screen message; US version: issue threat despite surrender (design inconsistency)
Imprisoned; require ice ball bomb destruction to release
Remain inert until discharged at island; trigger power-ups and fuel restoration upon discharge
Non-hostile; collision triggers hidden bonus messages revealing positive world-state outcome (alien surrender admission)

notes: Sqoon communicates world-state primarily through environmental submersion (flooded world = alien victory condition met) and through end-level narrative messages. Danger is signaled through fuel mechanics (time pressure) and enemy encounters. Factions are communicated implicitly through setting and explicit through distress signals and surrender messages. Regional differences in ending messages ('Ottoto' aliens in Japan vs. 'Neptunians' in US) reflect localization choices that affect political framing. The mermaid bonus messages serve as hidden world-state indicators. Victory condition requires both base destruction AND loop completion in US version, creating escalating difficulty manifestation. Game uses minimal text and relies heavily on visual/mechanical storytelling.
sources_used: tcrf - Sqoon regional differences, ending conditions, hidden messages
